title: Italian cuisine — overview
text: Italian cooking is intensely regional, built on simple combinations of high-quality ingredients: wheat pasta in the north, rice in the Po valley, olive oil, tomato, basil, garlic, and aged cheeses. The country was politically unified only in 1861, and most 'Italian food' Americans know is southern in origin.

title: Pasta basics
text: Pasta is divided into dried (durum wheat, mostly southern) and fresh egg pasta (mostly northern). Shapes are matched to sauces — long thin pasta to oil-based, tubes to chunky meat sauces, ridged shapes to thicker creamy ones. Al dente cooking leaves a slight bite.

title: Neapolitan pizza
text: Neapolitan pizza is a UNESCO-protected craft. The dough uses only flour, water, salt, and yeast, fermented slowly. It is hand-stretched, topped sparingly, and baked at around 900°F for 60 to 90 seconds in a wood-fired oven, producing a soft, charred crust.

title: French cuisine and the brigade
text: Classical French cuisine was codified by Escoffier in the late 19th century. He organized professional kitchens into the brigade system, with stations like sauce, fish, pastry, each headed by a chef de partie, all reporting to the executive chef. Most fine-dining kitchens still use it.

title: The five French mother sauces
text: Béchamel (milk thickened with roux), velouté (light stock plus roux), espagnole (brown stock plus roux), hollandaise (egg yolks emulsified with butter), and tomato. Hundreds of derivative sauces are built by adding flavorings to these five bases.

title: Japanese cuisine
text: Japanese cooking emphasizes seasonal ingredients, precise knife work, and restraint. A typical home meal is ichiju-sansai — one soup and three sides with rice. Dashi, made from kombu kelp and bonito flakes, is the umami backbone of countless dishes.

title: Sushi and rice
text: Sushi is defined by its vinegared rice (shari), not by raw fish. Edomae sushi developed in 19th-century Tokyo as fast street food using fish from the bay. Nigiri is a hand-pressed mound of rice topped with fish; maki is rolled in nori seaweed.

title: Ramen as a regional dish
text: Ramen arrived in Japan from China in the early 20th century and developed strong regional identities: rich pork-bone tonkotsu in Kyushu, miso-based ramen in Sapporo, soy-based shoyu in Tokyo, salt-based shio along the coasts. It is not traditional but modern.

title: Chinese cuisine — the eight traditions
text: Chinese cooking is often grouped into eight regional traditions: Sichuan, Cantonese, Shandong, Jiangsu, Zhejiang, Fujian, Hunan, and Anhui. Sichuan is known for chili and mouth-numbing peppercorn; Cantonese for steaming, freshness, and dim sum.

title: Wok cooking
text: The wok's high curved walls and conductive metal allow fast stir-frying over intense heat — a technique called wok hei, the smoky breath of the wok. Ingredients are cut small, pre-prepped, and tossed for less than a minute, preserving texture and color.

title: Sichuan and ma la
text: Sichuan cooking is built around ma la — numbing and spicy — from Sichuan peppercorn and dried chili. Signature dishes include mapo tofu, dan dan noodles, and hot pot. The combination of heat and tingling numbness is unique to the region.

title: Indian cuisine
text: Indian cooking spans dozens of regional traditions across the subcontinent. The north leans on wheat breads, dairy, and slow-cooked stews; the south on rice, lentils, coconut, and tamarind. Spices are typically bloomed in fat to release flavor before other ingredients are added.

title: Curry, defined
text: Curry is an English umbrella term for spiced, saucy dishes from many countries; it is not a single Indian concept. Indian curries use freshly ground spice blends called masalas; Thai curries use pounded fresh paste with coconut milk; Japanese curry is a thick, mild, roux-based stew.

title: Tandoor cooking
text: The tandoor is a cylindrical clay oven fired by wood or charcoal, reaching 900°F. Flatbreads like naan are slapped onto its walls; skewered meats hang above the coals. The intense heat sears the outside while keeping the inside juicy.

title: Thai cuisine
text: Thai cooking balances four flavors in nearly every dish: sweet, sour, salty, and spicy, often through palm sugar, lime, fish sauce, and chili. Pastes are pounded fresh in a mortar; fragrant herbs like Thai basil, kaffir lime leaf, and lemongrass are used generously.

title: Mexican cuisine and nixtamalization
text: Traditional Mexican cuisine is built on corn treated with alkali, a process called nixtamalization that loosens the hull, increases nutrition, and unlocks the corn flavor. The resulting masa is shaped into tortillas, tamales, and gorditas. The technique is over 3,000 years old.

title: Mole
text: Mole is a family of complex Mexican sauces from Oaxaca and Puebla, built from chiles, spices, seeds, nuts, dried fruit, and often chocolate. A single mole can use over 30 ingredients and take a full day to make. Mole negro, rojo, and amarillo are the most famous variants.

title: Taco regional styles
text: Tacos vary widely across Mexico: al pastor (Lebanese-influenced shawarma-style pork on a vertical spit) in Mexico City, carnitas (slow-braised pork) in Michoacán, fish tacos with cabbage and crema in Baja California. The flour tortilla is mostly a northern, border-state thing.

title: Spanish cuisine
text: Spanish food varies sharply by region: Galician seafood and octopus in the northwest, Basque pintxos in the north, Valencian rice and paella in the east, Andalusian gazpacho and fried fish in the south. Jamón Ibérico, cured for years from acorn-fed pigs, is a national obsession.

title: Tapas culture
text: Tapas are small dishes served with drinks, originally a slice of bread or cheese placed over a glass (tapa means lid) to keep flies out. Today they range from olives to elaborate seafood plates. In Basque country the equivalent is pintxos, typically pierced with a toothpick.

title: Middle Eastern mezze
text: Mezze are small shared plates eaten across the Levant, Turkey, and Greece — hummus, baba ganoush, tabbouleh, kibbeh, dolma, labneh. The format encourages slow, social eating; meals stretch for hours and dishes arrive in waves rather than courses.

title: Hummus and tahini
text: Hummus is a Levantine dip of cooked chickpeas blended with tahini (sesame paste), lemon juice, garlic, and olive oil. Tahini, made from toasted hulled sesame seeds, is also the base of halva and a sauce for shawarma. Origins of hummus are disputed across the region.

title: Korean cuisine and banchan
text: Korean meals are served with banchan, small side dishes — most famously kimchi, the fermented vegetable staple. A typical table has rice, soup or stew, and four or more banchan. Fermentation is central, from kimchi to soybean pastes like doenjang and gochujang.

title: Kimchi
text: Kimchi is a family of fermented vegetable preserves, most commonly napa cabbage with chili paste, garlic, ginger, fish sauce, and salted shrimp. It is fermented at room temperature for days then refrigerated for weeks, developing acidity, umami, and gentle carbonation.

title: Vietnamese cuisine
text: Vietnamese food emphasizes herbs, fresh vegetables, and balance — every meal includes raw greens. Pho, a beef bone broth noodle soup from the north, is the national dish; banh mi, a baguette sandwich, is a legacy of French colonization filled with Vietnamese flavors.

title: American barbecue regions
text: American barbecue splits into four main schools: Carolina (whole hog, vinegar and pepper sauce), Memphis (pork ribs, dry rubbed or with tomato sauce), Kansas City (any meat, thick sweet sauce), and Texas (beef brisket, salt and pepper bark, smoke from post oak).

title: Ethiopian cuisine and injera
text: Ethiopian meals are served on injera — a sour fermented flatbread made from teff flour, which doubles as both plate and utensil. Wats (spiced stews) of lentils, vegetables, or meat are spooned onto the injera; diners tear off pieces to scoop up the food.

title: Peruvian cuisine and ceviche
text: Peruvian cooking blends indigenous, Spanish, African, Chinese, and Japanese influences. Ceviche — raw fish cured briefly in citrus juice with chili, onion, and salt — is the national dish. The leftover citrus marinade, leche de tigre, is drunk on its own.

title: Fermentation across cuisines
text: Fermentation underlies staples worldwide: soy sauce and miso in Japan, kimchi in Korea, sauerkraut in Germany, cheese and wine in Europe, fish sauce across Southeast Asia, sourdough across the Mediterranean. It preserves food and develops umami, acidity, and complexity.

title: Knife skills and mise en place
text: Professional kitchens depend on mise en place — everything in its place — meaning all ingredients are cut, measured, and arranged before cooking starts. Knife skills (julienne, brunoise, chiffonade) are not just aesthetic; uniform cuts cook evenly, which matters most in fast techniques like stir-frying.
